name: Serranía de Cuenca and Alto Tajo Loop
name_original: Carreteras de Sierra de Albarracín y Montes Urales — circular desde Peralejos de las Truchas
description: This difficult 79.1-mile road cycling route through Sierra de Albarracín offers challenging climbs and stunning natural scenery.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Peralejos De Las Truchas are situated within the Alto Tajo Natural Park in Guadalajara, Castilla-La Mancha, Spain. The region features a diverse landscape characterized by deep canyons carved by rivers such as the Tajo, Hoz Seca, and Cabrillas, alongside extensive pine forests and mountains. Elevations range from approximately 1080 meters to 1701 meters above sea level, providing varied terrain with significant ascents and descents. This topography offers a challenging environment for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Peralejos De Las Truchas The most popular road cycling route is Serranía de Cuenca and Alto Tajo Loop, a 79.1 miles (127.3 km) trail that takes 6 hours 34 minutes to complete. This difficult route traverses the Sierra de Albarracín and the Alto Tajo region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto Tajo Park and Peralejos de las Truchas Loop, a difficult 43.7 miles (70.3 km) path. This route offers views within the Alto Tajo Natural Park, featuring dramatic canyons and varied terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Climb to the ports of Muela de San Juan and Puerto de Noguera — circular from Checa, a 57.4 miles (92.3 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain with significant climbs, often completed in about 4 hours 55 minutes. Road cycling around Peralejos De Las Truchas is defined by the canyons of the Tajo River, dense pine forests, and challenging mountain ascents. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Peralejos De Las Truchas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Peralejos De Las Truchas?
A: The region offers a diverse and challenging landscape for road cyclists. You'll encounter significant elevation changes, with altitudes ranging from approximately 1080 meters to 1701 meters above sea level. Routes often feature strenuous climbs and exhilarating descents through deep canyons carved by rivers like the Tajo, Hoz Seca, and Cabrillas, as well as dense pine forests and mountainous areas within the Alto Tajo Natural Park.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those seeking an easier ride?
A: While the area is known for its challenging terrain, with most routes rated as difficult, there is one route classified as moderate. This allows for a slightly less strenuous option while still enjoying the beautiful scenery of the Alto Tajo Natural Park.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I can see along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes often pass through or near impressive natural features. You can expect dramatic canyons, dense pine forests, and stunning viewpoints. Specific highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5430933" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salto de Poveda Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6498158" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna de Taravilla&lt;/a&gt;, and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5070873" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peralejos de las Truchas Cliffs&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also offer panoramic views from various miradores.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017680338/alto-tajo-park-and-peralejos-de-las-truchas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Tajo Park and Peralejos de las Truchas Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 43.7-mile (70.3 km) path offering varied terrain within the natural park. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017674219/climb-to-the-ports-of-muela-de-san-juan-and-puerto-de-noguera-circular-from-checa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Climb to the ports of Muela de San Juan and Puerto de Noguera — circular from Checa&lt;/a&gt;, a 57.4-mile (92.3 km) trail through mountainous terrain.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Peralejos De Las Truchas?
A: The region's climate makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summers can be warm, while winters may bring colder conditions and potential for snow at higher elevations, making some routes more challenging or inaccessible.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. For experienced riders seeking a significant challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018223931/serrania-de-cuenca-and-alto-tajo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Serranía de Cuenca and Alto Tajo Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 79.1-mile (127.3 km) trail that traverses the Sierra de Albarracín and the Alto Tajo region. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e963701107/birth-of-the-tajo-river-circular-through-the-montes-universales" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Birth of the Tajo River - circular through the Montes Universales&lt;/a&gt;, spanning over 140 km.

Q: Can I find routes that offer impressive viewpoints?
A: Yes, the mountainous topography and deep canyons provide numerous opportunities for breathtaking views. Miradores like El Rasón and Pie Molino are prominent viewpoints mentioned in the region research. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143506" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Megina and Chequilla Viewpoint loop from Pinilla de Molina&lt;/a&gt; is specifically designed to showcase scenic vistas.

Q: Are there options for parking near the road cycling routes?
A: While specific parking areas for each route are not detailed, Peralejos de las Truchas and surrounding villages typically offer public parking facilities. It's advisable to check local information for the most convenient starting points with parking availability for your chosen route.

Q: What is the average duration for road cycling routes in this area?
A: The duration varies significantly based on the route's length and difficulty. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1018223931/serrania-de-cuenca-and-alto-tajo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Serranía de Cuenca and Alto Tajo Loop&lt;/a&gt; typically takes around 6 hours and 34 minutes, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017680338/alto-tajo-park-and-peralejos-de-las-truchas-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Tajo Park and Peralejos de las Truchas Loop&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in about 3 hours and 50 minutes. Most difficult routes range from 3 to 7 hours.

Q: Are there any bridges or unique structures to see along the routes?
A: Yes, the region features interesting structures that are often integrated into cycling and hiking trails. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1121501" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Suspension bridge over the River Tajo&lt;/a&gt; is a notable landmark, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4785737" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tajo River Suspension Bridge, Parque Natural del Alto Tajo&lt;/a&gt;. Puente de Martinete, marking the provincial boundary, is also a significant point of interest.

Q: What makes road cycling in Peralejos De Las Truchas unique compared to other regions?
A: The unique appeal lies in its location within the Alto Tajo Natural Park, offering a pristine and tranquil environment. The dramatic canyons carved by three major rivers, combined with significant elevation changes and dense pine forests, create a cycling experience that is both physically demanding and visually spectacular, providing a true escape into nature.
tips: Orihuela del Tremedal has its roots in medieval times. It is believed that the town was founded in the 12th century, during the Reconquista, when Christians recovered territories from the Muslims.
The port is ideal for outdoor activities such as hiking and cycling, and offers spectacular views of the Tagus Valley and the surrounding pine forests.😎🚵🚴🌄

The CM-2119 road offers stunning views of the Serranía de Cuenca. This landscape is known for its unique rock formations, deep ravines and extensive pine forests. When travelling along this road, you can enjoy views towards the Sierra de Valdemeca and the Sierra de la Madera, especially in a southwesterly direction.🚴🚵🌄😎

The Cuervo River Spring is a dream place in the Serranía de Cuenca, Spain. This Natural Monument houses a main waterfall with a height of more than 20 meters, leaving those who contemplate it breathless. Here, the Cuervo River springs from a spring of travertine origin, flowing over limestone rock covered with moss, forming beautiful waterfalls called chorreras.
Beautiful road that is located between gorges resulting from the passage of the Tagus river over the years through this area. We also find the Puente del Martinete at the western end of the gorge.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete the Roads of Sierra de Albarracín and Montes Urales — circular from Peralejos de las Truchas?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling route typically takes around 6 hours and 35 minutes to complete. This duration is for the full 127-kilometer loop, assuming a steady pace.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and demands a very good level of fitness. With over 2,200 meters of elevation gain and a significant distance, it's best suited for experienced road cyclists looking for a demanding challenge.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on paved roads, but cyclists should be prepared for sections that might be less smooth or even unpaved, which can challenge road bikes. It traverses high-altitude terrain, often above 1,200 meters, featuring extensive pine forests, ravines (barrancos), and impressive reddish rock formations. The highest point reached is 1,780 meters.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience beautiful natural landscapes and pass through several notable points. Highlights include the charming settlement of Orihuela del Tremedal, the scenic Puerto del Portillo, and a stunning View of the Serranía de Cuenca. You can also visit the Source and waterfalls of the Cuervo River and the dramatic Peralejos de las Truchas Canyon.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Peralejos de las Truchas. Parking is generally available within the village. The starting point is also accessible by public transport, offering flexibility for cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the high-altitude terrain and potential for challenging weather, the best time to cycle this route is typically during the late spring, summer, and early autumn months. Avoid winter due to snow and ice, and be mindful of extreme heat in mid-summer.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route, especially within Parque Natural Serranía de Cuenca?
faqs.answer: The route passes through protected areas, including the Peripheral Protection Zone of the Alto Tajo Natural Park and the ZEC-ZEPA Alto Tajo, which are part of the broader Parque Natural Serranía de Cuenca. Generally, there are no specific entrance fees for cycling on public roads within these natural parks. However, always adhere to local regulations regarding protected areas, especially concerning waste disposal and respecting wildlife.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily on roads, making it less ideal for dogs to accompany cyclists directly, dogs are generally allowed in the surrounding natural areas on a leash. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are well-behaved and can handle the distance if not riding in a trailer. Always check specific park regulations for any restrictions.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other cycling paths or trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route overlaps with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of MontañasVacías (34%) and Las Tres Taifas (29%). You will also encounter parts of the GR 160 - Camino del Cid BTT (22%), [CIMA CU08] El Cubillo - El Portillo (13%), and the Camino Natural del Tajo (4%).

faqs.question: What gear should I bring for this road cycling adventure?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and varied terrain, a well-maintained road bike is essential. Consider tires that can handle potentially less smooth sections. Bring plenty of water and nutrition, as services may be sparse between villages. Appropriate cycling apparel for varying temperatures, a repair kit, and navigation tools are also highly recommended.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Peralejos de las Truchas, the starting and ending point, offers options for accommodation and places to eat. It's advisable to plan your stops for food and water in advance, as services can be limited in this remote region, especially outside of main villages.
